result:
Every morning, before any grand plan has a chance to assert itself, your day is negotiated by invisible compacts. You wake to an alarm synchronized by a radio signal you never asked for and can hardly refuse. The water that hits your face has crossed chlorination tanks and pressure zones, boundaries that were negotiated by engineers and plumbers and city councils. The phone in your hand knows where it is because of an agreement about time—atomic, arbitrary, exacting—that lets satellites and chips whisper about distance with a precision that smothers doubt. You swipe on a screen whose sensitivity is not only electrical but cultural: a default swipe direction agreed upon by interface designers thousands of miles away. You type a password on a keyboard laid out like a fossil of human fingers from a bygone age of levers and inked ribbon. And the words you read right now are ferried on standards and protocols that, if they were to take one afternoon off, would make your life and mine astonishingly strange.

The modern world is a symphony of such agreements, and the most life-changing of them are the ones that recede so far into the backdrop that we forget that anyone ever decided them. Standards are a kind of civics that lives in the muscle. There’s a reason that the miraculous feels mundane until the day it stops working: our brains are excellent at habituation but terrible at honorific memory. When your electricity is continuous, the grid is quiet. When your call connects, the phone network is forgettable. When your package arrives the next day with a mundane thump, logistics becomes a shrug. But under that shrug there are barcodes and pallets and databases and forklifts and the Global Trade Item Number and the Bill of Lading and the way the forklift and the pallet and the barcode and the shipping container all meet at right angles like a language spoken by wood and steel.

Consider the shipping container, which is as close as we have to a magic trick in metal. The container takes the uncontainable variety of human goods—lathes and lettuce, sweaters and solar panels—and enforces sameness at the interface. Inside the box, chaos can reign; outside, each corner is exactly where it should be. Ports became faster not because cranes became clever, but because boxes became equivalent. In software, we learned to stack protocols like those boxes; TCP rides on IP, HTTP rides on TCP, your message rides on HTTP, and your mood rides on your message. It’s turtles, or containers, all the way down. The outcome of this generalization is abundance and absurd fragility. We get bargains and specialization and late-night deliveries. We also get brittle dependencies and factory oceans and a world that has to hold its breath when a single canal is blocked by a single ship on a single windy day.

If you want to feel how standards distribute power, look at screws. The Phillips head screw was invented to cam out—to slip under high torque so that factory lines wouldn’t over-tighten. It was optimized for the efficiency of production, not the ease of the mechanic in the field. The Robertson screw, square drive, is beautiful in its hold but never conquered the United States, largely because of licensing and the American appetite for what you could buy from everyone. Torx heads, tamper-resistant variants, pentalobes inside phones: each design is an embodiment of a value judgment about who will be allowed to open what. A tri-wing screw in a gaming console is a quiet sentence: please don’t. Standards are not neutral; they are choices, repeated until they look like nature. When a device is glued shut, that glue is political. When a laptop uses standard fasteners, that too is a political kindness.

There is also a tyranny in long-standing decisions, one that hides beneath the banner of convenience. We live with QWERTY not because it is optimal for fingers, but because it is optimal for continuity. We remain divided between metric and imperial measurements because the cost of conversion and the inertia of infrastructure are heavier than the obviousness of base ten. Path dependence is cultural gravity. It is not just habit; it is investment in training, machines, documentation, spaced-out road signs, literal and figurative. Changing a population’s measuring system is like changing their jokes; you can mandate it, but meaning leaks out on the way. The success of a new standard often depends less on its brilliance and more on the ease with which it can be draped over the old one without too much draft.

Timekeeping itself is the most beguiling standard of all, the one that makes the others possible. Before trains ran to timetables across vast territories, noon was mostly when the sun crowed at its peak over your specific town. Standard time arrived so that iron schedules could line up in a way bodies could not. The introduction of zones was a triumph of friction reduction and a modest violence to place. Today, leap seconds are a skirmish between cosmic irregularity and computational hunger for continuity. Big systems sometimes smear these seconds across hours so that nothing trips over a discontinuity, and the sun and the servers come to a fragile truce. Meanwhile, our bodies still want dawn to be dawn, even if we are reading about dawn on a glass rectangle in a room lit at 9 p.m. like midday. There is a monthly romance between human circadian stubbornness and the strict tyrannies of synchronization.

A favorite fiction of the internet age is that things are “in the cloud,” which suggests elevation and softness. Most of what you think of as the cloud is quite literally mud. Fiber optic cables are threaded across ocean floors by ships in weather that cares nothing for your notifications. Landing stations sit in unglamorous buildings on quiet coasts guarded not by lasers but by fences and fans. Fishing trawlers snag lines and blink whole islands into quiet. There is redundancy, yes; there is also a rude lesson in geography and geopolitics. The cloud is full of addresses and contracts and insurance, and when something breaks, someone with a wrench and a hard hat and a thermos has to go fix it while the wind pushes everything sideways. If you need a prayer for civilization, it might be: may there always be coffee and wrenches.

The best designs acknowledge that reality and hide complexity in places where it is gentle to hide. Layering is the human trick. We reason in chunks. We put shelves in closets and universes into directories. A nice interface takes a hostile underlying world and pretends that it is coherent, and usually that pretense is generous. A cash register reports “chip malfunction” because to say “your card’s applet cryptography had a hiccup during the dynamic data authentication handshake” would be psychologically useless. A document format decides how to represent a paragraph and lets you ignore the drudgery of kerning. APIs massage intricacies so developers can build things without having to become physicists. But there is a tax to every abstraction, a kind of interest payment on ignorance. Sometimes you need to open the hood. Then you discover that the hood latch is where it is because of headlight geometry and crash standards and a factory jig last updated in 2009, and you are suddenly an archaeologist.

Language itself is a kind of infrastructure that took a perplexing turn when it met computers. Unicode put a map over the writing of the world, and whenever there is a map, there is a fight about what counts as a mountain. Scripts are politics. Encoding Han characters involved debates about equivalence that carry heavier knives than most programmers realize. Emoji are a metonymy of culture wars, smuggled in as faces and tacos. When you select a skin tone or a family arrangement, you are participating in a standard that decides how humans appear to each other. Keyboard layouts summon national identities. Directionality rules wrestle with Hebrew and Arabic; normalization rules argue with accents; zero-width joiners perform an acrobatic diplomacy between adjacent forms. Behind every cleanly rendered sentence sits a coalition of committees and proposals and vendors, and behind them sit centuries of scribes and strokes and schools of calligraphy.

The shape of user experience involves theater as much as it involves logic. Progress bars, for instance, are often lies told with good intentions. They do not show you how much of the job has truly been done; they show you that the job is alive and that you are not forgotten. Many elevators’ close-door buttons are unconnected, placebo treatments for impatience that simply instruct you to act civilized and wait. Crosswalk buttons sometimes do nothing and sometimes do something, depending on the hour and the intersection’s control logic, depending on whether the system is running on a fixed program or a sensor-driven adaptation. You are allowed to press a button to declare your desire. Whether the system honors it is a separate question. Our expectation of agency is part of the system design, just as much as any bolt or wire.

There’s a term for this thoughtful backgrounding of complexity: calm technology. The best systems have a way of living at the edge of your attention until you summon them. We used to have bells that told us noon, whistles that marked shift changes, lights that signaled groceries were open. A good notification ecology has frequencies and amplitudes as carefully considered as those of a neighborhood’s soundscape. There should be levels: the gentle nudge, the polite alarm, the rare klaxon. If everything's urgent, nothing is urgent; if everything is ambient, nothing is salient. A washing machine that sings a bar of a melody to say it’s done is charming the first time and a microaggression on the fiftieth. To build humane complexity, you have to love boredom. You design for the long encounters, for the people who will live with your product daily, not just the people who will gasp at it once.

A tricky truth of our time is that maintenance, not invention, is the greater part of what keeps the world livable. We celebrate breakthrough moments because they compress neatly into headlines and slides. But the person who walks a pipeline checking for leaks, who greases the bearings in a wind turbine, who applies the patch that closes a software vulnerability at two in the morning, is the protagonist of our survival story. Rust never sleeps; neither do bugs. In software, we talk about tech debt as if debt is a metaphor, but it is not. It accrues interest. It narrows your options. It makes cleverness into obligation. There’s a discipline to paying it down that has nothing to do with thrilling firsts and everything to do with showing up. We need a culture that confers status on the caretakers, not only the catapulters.

There’s an old electrician’s aphorism: electricity is lazy, always looking for the shortest path to ground. But it’s also ambitious in its reach. The electrical grid is a nearly unimaginable consensus about simultaneity. In many regions, frequency must stay so close to 50 or 60 hertz that it barely wobbles or else you blow transformers and melt what should not melt. This requires real-time dance moves among power plants and loads: if a factory starts up, a generator somewhere else yawns a little wider; if a storm drops lines, an algorithm somewhere decides what to sacrifice. There are spinning reserves, black start plants, phasor measurement units telling the story of the grid's heartbeat. We flip a switch, a light comes on, and we barely notice the opera that made it so. We only notice when the opera stops and the refrigerator mutters to a halt and the ice cream becomes an emergency.

Queues are another kind of hidden philosophy. Who gets served first? In what order? Do we prioritize the person with the simple ask to clear the line, or the person who has been waiting the longest? Banks and airports and call centers run these experiments every day, often without admitting that they are running them. Queue disciplines are moral positions with economic consequences. First-come, first-served feels fair but can be inefficient. Shortest job first feels efficient but can strand the complicated forever. Triage is necessary in emergencies but in peacetime it can harden into cruelty. And while you are on hold, the music is a design element. The soothing voice reminding you that “your call is important to us” is not only a voice; it is a contract, a statement of how an institution sees your patience. Even the choice to interrupt your waiting with occasional updates is a bet about your psychology. We ought to make these bets explicit.

Right to repair sits at the crossroads of screws and queues and ethics. Devices get thinner and smoother, harder to open, easier to discard. Manufacturers talk about safety and water resistance and design sleekness, and those claims are often true. They also like control. The choice between a glued battery and a bracketed one is a choice about who gets to decide when your device dies. Enabling independent repair is messy. People break things and blame you; supply chains for parts become their own micro-industries; counterfeiters exploit the margins. And yet there’s dignity in being able to keep your tools alive. A farmer with a broken tractor and a proprietary error code is not a corner case; they are a story about how far the culture of convenience will go to dominate the culture of competence. It is good to make things that open and shut without violence, that welcome a screwdriver like a friend.

Memory is as much a standard as electricity. What we keep and how we keep it will shape how the future reads us. File formats carry time bombs. Open formats with public documentation are boring in the best way; proprietary formats are thrilling until the company goes away. The digital decay called bit rot is often a matter of orphaned choices: a codec nobody carries forward, a library no one compiles anymore, an encryption key misplaced. Print an acid-free paper book and barring fire or flood it will last a century. Put your photo album into a social network and you are a product manager’s reorg away from nostalgic oblivion. Archiving is not a hoarder’s vice; it is intergenerational courtesy. The best institutions understand that backups are only real if someone actually restores them, and that the difference between a thriving archive and a roomy bin is the quality of its metadata.

The hidden life of addresses is a quiet wonder. To move molecules to the right people, systems need to know where to go. Street names carry history and politics. Numbering schemes warp around rivers and railroads and old city walls. Postcodes lie about where you are because their first job is to route letters, not to please geographers. On the network, addresses are even slipperier. A hostname is friendlier than an IP, and DNS is the benevolent yellow pages that tell your browser where to knock; when DNS is hijacked or broken, the entire glowing overlay of the internet evaporates, and you realize your sense of ubiquity was always borrowed. Naming things is one of the hard problems in computing because naming things is one of the hard problems in life. We argue about what to call the same place, the same person, the same concept, because to name is to claim.

The systems that treat us most kindly admit uncertainty and prepare for it. Reliability engineers speak of error budgets, an explicit allowance that says you cannot chase perfection without starving experimentation. The right number of nines in your uptime is the one that buys you forgiveness without bankrupting you. Graceful degradation is not a hedge; it is an ethic. When something fails, can a smaller version of it keep going? Can the application say, “I don’t know everything, but I know enough to be helpful”? Can the bus link keep running with reduced frequency? Can the hospital base its redundancies on the cruel mathematics of what must not fail? The trick is to be transparent enough to keep trust while being resilient enough to keep moving. Honesty is a reliability tool. So is humility.

If you want to feel the stakes of all this, spend a day in an airport not as a passenger but as a student. Watch the signage: it is a language of arrows and colors whose grammar must communicate across tongues and timetables. Watch the ground crew ballet with wands and vehicles, each cart arriving at its place because someone, somewhere, argued about where a door should go on a fuselage fifteen years ago. Listen to the utter blandness of most announcements, honed carefully to be heard without excess adrenaline. Then watch a delay ripple, and see how quickly the choreography starts to lurch. When does the system tell the truth about how long you’ll be there? When does it make soothing noises? The airport is a condensed city that runs on fuel and promises and the belief that everything will eventually line up again. It is a temple to both standardization and fatigue.

Alongside the big networked beasts, there are micro-infrastructures that keep the intimate beats of life. A neighborhood library’s catalog system is a marvel of linking: bibliographic records, subject headings, circulation limits, fines or their abolition, renewals and interlibrary loans, the soft law of local need. The promise that your book will be there when you return is backed by a pledge that a tiny army of shelvers and scanners and silent notices will coax chaos back into alphabetical order. The beauty of that system is not only that it works, but that it invites you to use it. Public goods are a kind of standard—an agreement that we will meet each other here under these rules to do something that matters and that no one person could do alone. The water fountain at the park; the outlet in the corner of the café; the timetable posted at the bus stop; each is a standard wrapped in kindness.

These ideas can sound lofty until the day an assumption breaks under your feet. Maybe you moved to a town where left turns work differently, and you found yourself creeping into an intersection with the wrong expectations. Maybe an update borked your documents and you learned that you are only as safe as your last export to plaintext. Maybe you learned how much work it was to set up a new appliance because a decade of familiarity died with its old interface. Maybe you inherited a workshop where every previous owner used a different thread standard and now every bolt mocks you. Discomfort teaches you to be a gentler standard-setter. You learn to leave breadcrumbs in your designs for the future you that forgot what you meant. You make the naming scheme that will read like an apology or a hug a year from now. You spare a thought for whoever will come after you with a wrench.

So much about a better future could be framed as an adjustment in our aesthetic. We put a lot of glamour on the new thing, the shiny thing, the unboxed thing. What if we put more glamour on the well-maintained thing, the annotated thing, the mended thing? What if the sexiest launch video was a time-lapse of ten years of uptime with attentive updates and an owner’s manual that reads like a story? What if our notion of innovation was less about what’s possible and more about what will still be true in a decade? The boring path is graceful. The elegant screw fits easily, resists stripping, forgives misalignment. The good standard aims not to dominate but to enable—the way a good host keeps a party smooth without making it about themselves. That ethos can suffuse electrical codes and data schemas and park benches alike.

Of course, these systems can wound. Surveillance is an infrastructure that feels like a network until it feels like a net. Algorithms that route cars through neighborhoods can turn quiet streets into conveyor belts, translating cleverness into fumes. Subsidized inefficiencies can hide in standards that were written when the facts were different; they become sacred because no one wants to open the hood and face the dust. This is why governance matters. Who sits in the room where a specification is written will show up later in who can afford to comply. Processes that welcome the small player, review evidence in the light, and admit error without humiliation will build standards that age more gracefully. We want committees that write like gardeners, not generals.

Underneath the complexity there is a startlingly simple human practice: coordination. The hardest part of doing things together is discovering what we mean by together. The phrase rough consensus and running code describes a kind of civic realism. We will not all get everything we want in the standard, but we will get enough to ship something that works, and we will make it sufficiently satisfying that we don’t resent it. Then we will iterate. We will keep the tests that prevent regression as our common law. We will accept that some earliest decisions will haunt us and try to make them haunt us politely. We will document why we did this weird thing on line 473. We will try, when the moment arrives, to deprecate with compassion. We will write migration guides like letters to our future friends.

It’s an odd comfort to imagine a historian, a few generations forward, reading the mid-21st century not just as wars and elections but as a quilt of quiet agreements. They will find records of the day we stopped using leap seconds, or the hour the last undersea cable was laid in copper before we went fully to glass, or the administrative nudge that brought repair parts to rural clinics in two days instead of ten. They might quote policy memos, but they might also quote a product manual for a dishwasher that delighted a decade of households with an honest diagram and an easy-to-replace seal. They will see the world we built in the floss of everyday objects and the threads of how we cared for them. A civilization is the sum of what it makes easy and what it refuses to allow.

There’s a delight, too, in noticing the quiet mercies in our own days. Thank you to the person who numbered the aisles and drew a little map on the endcap. Thank you to the signal maintainer who aligned the platform announcement clocks to within a half-second. Thank you to the people who made your phone’s airplane mode really turn off what it should. Thank you to the neighbor who fixed the squeaky hinge on the gate everyone uses. These are small acts, but they are also strategy: they keep the seams where they belong. They let attention be spent on the right problems, on the joys and frictions where human judgment matters most. To notice them is to keep faith with the fact that complexity can be generous in the right hands.

Maybe the most practical habit to adopt in this line is simple: when you meet a system, ask who it was built to be kind to. If the answer is “the person who made it,” that’s a start; they will be more likely to maintain it if they like touching it. Better yet if the answer is “the person who will have to fix it in the rain.” Best of all if the answer is “the person who will use it without thinking about it, on a day when they are a little tired and late and sad.” Standards, infrastructure, maintenance—these are not only technical practices. They are a way to hold each other up through a thousand ordinary mornings. We will always argue about what to make, and that is fine. Let’s also keep a running project of how to make it so that more of us can live with it well.

Manufacturers talk about safety and water resistance and design sleekness, and those claims are often true. They also like control. The choice between a glued battery and a bracketed one is a choice about who gets to decide when your device dies. Enabling independent repair is messy. People break things and blame you; supply chains for parts become their own micro-industries; counterfeiters exploit the margins. And yet there’s dignity in being able to keep your tools alive. A farmer with a broken tractor and a proprietary error code is not a corner case; they are a story about how far the culture of convenience will go to dominate the culture of competence. It is good to make things that open and shut without violence, that welcome a screwdriver like a friend.

Memory is as much a standard as electricity. What we keep and how we keep it will shape how the future reads us. File formats carry time bombs. Open formats with public documentation are boring in the best way; proprietary formats are thrilling until the company goes away. The digital decay called bit rot is often a matter of orphaned choices: a codec nobody carries forward, a library no one compiles anymore, an encryption key misplaced. Print an acid-free paper book and barring fire or flood it will last a century. Put your photo album into a social network and you are a product manager’s reorg away from nostalgic oblivion. Archiving is not a hoarder’s vice; it is intergenerational courtesy. The best institutions understand that backups are only real if someone actually restores them, and that the difference between a thriving archive and a roomy bin is the quality of its metadata.

The hidden life of addresses is a quiet wonder. To move molecules to the right people, systems need to know where to go. Street names carry history and politics. Numbering schemes warp around rivers and railroads and old city walls. Postcodes lie about where you are because their first job is to route letters, not to please geographers. On the network, addresses are even slipperier. A hostname is friendlier than an IP, and DNS is the benevolent yellow pages that tell your browser where to knock; when DNS is hijacked or broken, the entire glowing overlay of the internet evaporates, and you realize your sense of ubiquity was always borrowed. Naming things is one of the hard problems in computing because naming things is one of the hard problems in life. We argue about what to call the same place, the same person, the same concept, because to name is to claim.

The systems that treat us most kindly admit uncertainty and prepare for it. Reliability engineers speak of error budgets, an explicit allowance that says you cannot chase perfection without starving experimentation. The right number of nines in your uptime is the one that buys you forgiveness without bankrupting you. Graceful degradation is not a hedge; it is an ethic. When something fails, can a smaller version of it keep going? Can the application say, “I don’t know everything, but I know enough to be helpful”? Can the bus link keep running with reduced frequency? Can the hospital base its redundancies on the cruel mathematics of what must not fail? The trick is to be transparent enough to keep trust while being resilient enough to keep moving. Honesty is a reliability tool. So is humility.

If you want to feel the stakes of all this, spend a day in an airport not as a passenger but as a student. Watch the signage: it is a language of arrows and colors whose grammar must communicate across tongues and timetables. Watch the ground crew ballet with wands and vehicles, each cart arriving at its place because someone, somewhere, argued about where a door should go on a fuselage fifteen years ago. Listen to the utter blandness of most announcements, honed carefully to be heard without excess adrenaline. Then watch a delay ripple, and see how quickly the choreography starts to lurch. When does the system tell the truth about how long you’ll be there? When does it make soothing noises? The airport is a condensed city that runs on fuel and promises and the belief that everything will eventually line up again. It is a temple to both standardization and fatigue.

Alongside the big networked beasts, there are micro-infrastructures that keep the intimate beats of life. A neighborhood library’s catalog system is a marvel of linking: bibliographic records, subject headings, circulation limits, fines or their abolition, renewals and interlibrary loans, the soft law of local need. The promise that your book will be there when you return is backed by a pledge that a tiny army of shelvers and scanners and silent notices will coax chaos back into alphabetical order. The beauty of that system is not only that it works, but that it invites you to use it. Public goods are a kind of standard—an agreement that we will meet each other here under these rules to do something that matters and that no one person could do alone. The water fountain at the park; the outlet in the corner of the café; the timetable posted at the bus stop; each is a standard wrapped in kindness.

These ideas can sound lofty until the day an assumption breaks under your feet. Maybe you moved to a town where left turns work differently, and you found yourself creeping into an intersection with the wrong expectations. Maybe an update borked your documents and you learned that you are only as safe as your last export to plaintext. Maybe you learned how much work it was to set up a new appliance because a decade of familiarity died with its old interface. Maybe you inherited a workshop where every previous owner used a different thread standard and now every bolt mocks you. Discomfort teaches you to be a gentler standard-setter. You learn to leave breadcrumbs in your designs for the future you that forgot what you meant. You make the naming scheme that will read like an apology or a hug a year from now. You spare a thought for whoever will come after you with a wrench.

So much about a better future could be framed as an adjustment in our aesthetic. We put a lot of glamour on the new thing, the shiny thing, the unboxed thing. What if we put more glamour on the well-maintained thing, the annotated thing, the mended thing? What if the sexiest launch video was a time-lapse of ten years of uptime with attentive updates and an owner’s manual that reads like a story? What if our notion of innovation was less about what’s possible and more about what will still be true in a decade? The boring path is graceful. The elegant screw fits easily, resists stripping, forgives misalignment. The good standard aims not to dominate but to enable—the way a good host keeps a party smooth without making it about themselves. That ethos can suffuse electrical codes and data schemas and park benches alike.

Of course, these systems can wound. Surveillance is an infrastructure that feels like a network until it feels like a net. Algorithms that route cars through neighborhoods can turn quiet streets into conveyor belts, translating cleverness into fumes. Subsidized inefficiencies can hide in standards that were written when the facts were different; they become sacred because no one wants to open the hood and face the dust. This is why governance matters. Who sits in the room where a specification is written will show up later in who can afford to comply. Processes that welcome the small player, review evidence in the light, and admit error without humiliation will build standards that age more gracefully. We want committees that write like gardeners, not generals.

Underneath the complexity there is a startlingly simple human practice: coordination. The hardest part of doing things together is discovering what we mean by together. The phrase rough consensus and running code describes a kind of civic realism. We will not all get everything we want in the standard, but we will get enough to ship something that works, and we will make it sufficiently satisfying that we don’t resent it. Then we will iterate. We will keep the tests that prevent regression as our common law. We will accept that some earliest decisions will haunt us and try to make them haunt us politely. We will document why we did this weird thing on line 473. We will try, when the moment arrives, to deprecate with compassion. We will write migration guides like letters to our future friends.

It’s an odd comfort to imagine a historian, a few generations forward, reading the mid-21st century not just as wars and elections but as a quilt of quiet agreements. They will find records of the day we stopped using leap seconds, or the hour the last undersea cable was laid in copper before we went fully to glass, or the administrative nudge that brought repair parts to rural clinics in two days instead of ten. They might quote policy memos, but they might also quote a product manual for a dishwasher that delighted a decade of households with an honest diagram and an easy-to-replace seal. They will see the world we built in the floss of everyday objects and the threads of how we cared for them. A civilization is the sum of what it makes easy and what it refuses to allow.

There’s a delight, too, in noticing the quiet mercies in our own days. Thank you to the person who numbered the aisles and drew a little map on the endcap. Thank you to the signal maintainer who aligned the platform announcement clocks to within a half-second. Thank you to the people who made your phone’s airplane mode really turn off what it should. Thank you to the neighbor who fixed the squeaky hinge on the gate everyone uses. These are small acts, but they are also strategy: they keep the seams where they belong. They let attention be spent on the right problems, on the joys and frictions where human judgment matters most. To notice them is to keep faith with the fact that complexity can be generous in the right hands.

Maybe the most practical habit to adopt in this line is simple: when you meet a system, ask who it was built to be kind to. If the answer is “the person who made it,” that’s a start; they will be more likely to maintain it if they like touching it. Better yet if the answer is “the person who will have to fix it in the rain.” Best of all if the answer is “the person who will use it without thinking about it, on a day when they are a little tired and late and sad.” Standards, infrastructure, maintenance—these are not only technical practices. They are a way to hold each other up through a thousand ordinary mornings. We will always argue about what to make, and that is fine. Let’s also keep a running project of how to make it so that more of us can live with it well.
